### U.S. Department of Energy - Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy

#### Alternative Fuels Data Center

[EERE](http://www.eere.energy.gov/) » [AFDC](https://afdc.energy.gov/) » [Fuels &Vehicles](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels) » [Hydrogen](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen)

*   [Printable Version](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-stations#printable)

[More in this section...](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-stations#left_nav)

*   [Hydrogen Basics](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-basics)
*   [Benefits & Considerations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-benefits)
*   [Stations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-stations)
*   [Vehicles](https://afdc.energy.gov/vehicles/fuel-cell)
*   [Laws & Incentives](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/laws/HY)

Hydrogen Fueling Stations
=========================

![Image 2: Photo of a hydrogen fueling station.](https://afdc.energy.gov/vite/assets/photo-hydrogen-stations-CjYaUFQj.jpg)

The increased number of retail hydrogen fueling locations in select markets is supporting the initial rollout of fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs). Manufacturers including Hyundai and Toyota are currently offering production FCEVs for sale or lease to customers in markets where hydrogen fuel is available, primarily in California. With careful planning, the focus has been to add hydrogen fuel at existing gasoline stations covering regions in northern California near San Francisco and southern California near Los Angeles and San Diego, with additional connector and destination stations.

Mobile hydrogen fuelers, where liquefied or compressed hydrogen and dispensing equipment is stored onboard a trailer, are also being developed to support the expansion of hydrogen infrastructure. These efforts are giving early FCEV adopters confidence that they can drive normally and have access to hydrogen fuel wherever they go within these regions. Efforts are also underway to expand hydrogen fueling locations in Hawaii and across the East Coast, with other markets expected to develop as consumer demand increases. In 2024, there were [54 retail stations](https://afdc.energy.gov/stations/#/find/nearest?country=US&fuel=HY&lpg_secondary=true) available nationwide, mostly concentrated in California. In addition to the public, retail stations—which are available for all light-duty FCEVs, accept payment at the point of sale, and do not require special training to access—there are also private stations supporting fleets and some primarily used for demonstration and/or research. As the demand for FCEVs grows, the business case for building more stations with higher capacities improves and the volume in production of station components will bring station costs down.

The rollout of heavy-duty hydrogen trucks, such as line-haul trucks, will necessitate very large stations compared to light-duty needs. The increase in production and distribution of hydrogen for these stations could improve efficiency and utilization of expensive capital equipment, leading to lower fuel costs per kilogram, benefiting both heavy- and light-duty customers.

Hydrogen infrastructure is also developing for buses, medium-duty fleets, and material handling equipment. Unlike the public consumer stations for FCEVs that need multiple locations to cover wherever the consumer may travel, private fleet fueling stations require fewer locations or even just a central location to meet a specific fleet's needs.

[Station Locations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-locations)
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Find [hydrogen fueling stations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-locations) by location or along a route. Use the advanced filters to search for private and planned stations, as well as hydrogen fueling stations to match certain search criteria.

Infrastructure Development
--------------------------

The availability of stations providing reasonably priced hydrogen in places where vehicles will be deployed remains a key challenge to the adoption of fuel cell vehicle technology. To address this challenge, the U.S. Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) established a [National Alternative Fuels Corridors](https://www.fhwa.dot.gov/environment/alternative_fuel_corridors/) grant program in 2022. In consultation with the U.S. Department of Energy, FHWA awards designation status to selected corridors to meet the national goal of achieving strategic deployment of hydrogen fueling infrastructure in the designated hydrogen corridors.

The Bipartisan Infrastructure Law includes $7 billion in funding for seven [Regional Clean Hydrogen Hubs (H2Hubs)](https://www.energy.gov/oced/regional-clean-hydrogen-hubs-0) to accelerate the commercial-scale deployment of low-cost, clean hydrogen. Additional funds from recipients will leverage nearly $50 billion to grow a clean hydrogen production, storage, distribution, and end-use network across the country. These H2Hubs will include projects focused on deployment of infrastructure to enable hydrogen vehicle adoption for fleets and the public.

Building hydrogen fueling infrastructure can be expensive, which is why the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 incentivizes projects with low lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions by providing a tax credit, 45V, of up to $3 per kilogram of hydrogen. This comprehensive set of rules for taxpayers to claim the 45V credit provides certainty to current and future hydrogen producers to stabilize and expand the market for their products. Learn more about this [clean hydrogen production credit](https://www.irs.gov/credits-deductions/clean-hydrogen-production-credit).

As of 2024, there are 54 open retail hydrogen stations in the United States. Additionally, there are over 20 stations in various stages of planning or construction. Most of the existing and planned stations are in California and one in Hawaii. As the market expands, [hydrogen fueling stations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen_locations.html) will be matched with vehicle rollout as both grow together. Customers are expected to have similar experiences at hydrogen fueling stations as at gasoline stations, with most hydrogen dispensers being added at existing gasoline stations.

The [Alternative Fueling Station Locator](https://afdc.energy.gov/stations/#/find/nearest) allows users to search for public and private hydrogen fueling stations. Suggest new hydrogen stations for inclusion in the Station Locator using the [Submit New Station form](https://afdc.energy.gov/stations/#/station/new).

### Safety, Codes, and Standards

Many of the hydrogen safety codes and standards today are based on practices from the chemical and aerospace industries. DOE is coordinating the efforts of codes and standards organizations to develop more robust codes and standards that ensure the safe use of hydrogen for transportation and stationary applications. One of the outputs of this effort is [NFPA 2](https://www.nfpa.org/codes-and-standards/all-codes-and-standards/list-of-codes-and-standards/detail?code=2), a harmonized national standard for hydrogen vehicle infrastructure.

Learn more about hydrogen [safety, codes, and standards](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/safety-codes-and-standards) from the Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies Office.

### Demonstration Projects

In addition to the technical challenges being addressed through [research and development](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-research), there are obstacles to successful implementation of hydrogen fueling infrastructure that can be addressed only by integrating the components into complete systems. DOE is developing and testing complete system solutions that validate integrated hydrogen and fuel cell technologies for transportation, infrastructure, and electric generation in a systems context under real-world operating conditions.

Learn more about [systems analysis](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/systems-analysis) and [technology validation](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/technology-validation) from the Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Technologies Office. The National Renewable Energy Laboratory also has information about [hydrogen and fuel cell technology validation](https://www.nrel.gov/hydrogen/technology-validation.html).

The State of Hydrogen Fueling Stations in the U.S.
==================================================

No category / October 3, 2024

![Image 8](blob:http://localhost/6fd698f6d7701925acda723b08644738)![Image 9: The State of Hydrogen Fueling Stations in the U.S.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/zlfgolrr/production/403d9be8685c469be2dbe383e2c5687974a3d73d-1405x746.png?w=1405&h=746&auto=format)

**The State of Hydrogen Fueling Stations in the U.S.**

Hydrogen power has the potential to reshape the U.S. economy while supporting the adoption of clean energy here and around the world. More specifically, [hydrogen fuel is expected to play a critical role in infrastructure](https://www.fastechus.com/blog/hydrogen-fuel-cells-in-infrastructure-development), manufacturing, and other industries across the U.S. in the years to come. But hydrogen’s own infrastructure needs to be in place first so everyone can reap the benefits that hydrogen provides.

Leading this infrastructure development is the production and maintenance of hydrogen fueling stations. Below, we’ll provide an overview of what these stations are and how they work. We’ll also dive into their current status, future projections, and implications for hydrogen power.

**What are Hydrogen Fueling Stations?**
---------------------------------------

Hydrogen as an alternative energy source is most commonly used in hydrogen fuel cells, which power hydrogen fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) among many other use cases. Vehicles and other machines that use these cells require hydrogen fueling stations to replenish them, much like their gasoline- or diesel-powered equivalents do. The way these stations look and work mirrors their conventional fuel counterparts, with storage tanks for hydrogen fuel either produced on-site or carted in, as well as individual hose lines that pump fuel into a vehicle’s receptacle.

As the Alternative Fuels Data Center (AFDC) [overview of hydrogen fueling stations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-stations) notes, there are also mobile hydrogen fueling stations being developed, which will increase the availability of hydrogen fuel across the country. Another positive development in this regard is the creation of [hydrogen hubs](https://www.fastechus.com/blog/hydrogen-hubs) that streamline hydrogen production, storage, transportation, and utilization.

**Current Hydrogen Station Infrastructure in the U.S.**
-------------------------------------------------------

Stations are arguably the most important part of hydrogen infrastructure, as they’re the point at which hydrogen fuel reaches consumers. Efforts to increase stations and station access are driving overall hydrogen infrastructure development and hydrogen fuel adoption.

According to the AFDC’s [alternative fuel station counts resource](https://afdc.energy.gov/stations/states?count=total&include_temporarily_unavailable=false&date=), there are currently 74 total hydrogen fueling stations spread across the U.S., the vast majority of which are in California:

*   California: 54 retail and 8 non-retail stations
*   Colorado: 1 non-retail station
*   Hawaii: 1 retail and 1 non-retail station
*   Massachusetts: 1 non-retail station
*   Michigan: 2 non-retail stations
*   New York: 1 non-retail station
*   Ohio: 2 non-retail stations
*   Texas: 1 non-retail station
*   Virginia: 1 non-retail station
*   Washington: 1 non-retail station

Retail stations are those that are fully available to the public and accept payments directly at the point of purchase. Non-retail stations are only usable by certain clientele with specific access criteria like specialized training or membership in a given company, fleet, or other organization. Some of these are strictly _private_ (e.g., 5 of California’s 8 non-retail stations), and their purposes vary from the relatively uniform public stations—e.g., some are for demonstrations or research.

The AFDC also provides a [dynamic map of hydrogen fueling stations](https://afdc.energy.gov/fuels/hydrogen-locations#/find/nearest?fuel=HY) across the U.S. and Canada, so hydrogen fuel users can plan a trip based on where they’ll be able to fuel efficiently.

**Opportunities for Growth in Hydrogen Infrastructure**
-------------------------------------------------------

Hydrogen stations are concentrated in California, and the state should see more development in the near future. A [newly approved hydrogen hub in California](https://www.fastechus.com/blog/california-hydrogen-hub) will allocate $1.2B in federal funding to the Alliance for Renewable Clean Hydrogen Energy Systems (ARCHES) and generate an expected $11.2 billion from private investors. But California’s not the only place that should see increased hydrogen infrastructure development in the coming years; states with proof-of-concept stations are poised to build more as demand for hydrogen fuel increases.

Demand should also increase, as initiatives like the Department of Energy’s (DOE) [H2USA](https://www.energy.gov/eere/fuelcells/h2usa) are increasing public-private partnerships between federal/local agencies and auto manufacturers.

Beyond consumer FCEVs, another major sector hydrogen fuel could take over is heavy-duty industrial vehicles. Recent advancements such as powerful and efficient [hydrogen-powered semi trucks](https://www.fastechus.com/blog/hydrogen-powered-semi-trucks-facts) are paving the way for greater hydrogen adoption at scale across the country.

**Challenges Facing Hydrogen Fueling Stations**
-----------------------------------------------

Despite its overall potential to optimize energy efficiency and support environmental preservation, there are both [benefits and potential downsides to hydrogen fuel](https://www.fastechus.com/blog/challenges-and-advantages-of-hydrogen-fuel).

For instance, some hydrogen production methods are energy-intensive and/or reliant on fossil fuels. In addition, production and distribution are presently relatively expensive, and the lack of existing trade networks and supply lines creates a vicious cycle of building new ones. Better and more dependable hydrogen-powered cars and trucks will also necessitate more infrastructure.

**The Future of Hydrogen Fueling Stations in the U.S.**
-------------------------------------------------------

Looking ahead, hydrogen stations are expected to evolve and increase in number as demand grows. The global race toward [natural hydrogen](https://www.fastechus.com/blog/natural-hydrogen) and greater efficiency than current production standards provide will continue to drive demand. Consumers want efficient, eco-friendly energy, and hydrogen fuel is one of the best ways to achieve that—so long as there is infrastructure.

This is a big reason the [hydrogen fueling station market](https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/reports/hydrogen-fueling-station-market) projects to grow at 17.5% CAGR over the next 10 years and hit $2.3B by 2034, up from its current value of $454.1M. These are conservative estimates—the market grew at 20.6% CAGR from 2019 to 2023.

Whatever rate the market grows at, it will be driven by further advancements in hydrogen fuel technology, such as more cost-effective and accessible production, storage, and distribution.

These are exactly the kinds of advancements FASTECH’s [hydrogen infrastructure design and construction](https://www.fastechus.com/expertise/alternative-energy/hydrogen) services support. We’re believers in the promise of hydrogen fuel, and we help businesses prepare for and take advantage of the efficiency and other benefits it provides.

To learn more about how we can help your organization, [get in touch today](https://www.fastechus.com/contact/)!

enriched_points.point_title: Number of hydrogen fueling stations in the U.S.
enriched_points.expanded_content: As of 2024, there are 54 operational hydrogen fueling stations in the U.S., primarily concentrated in California, with more planned to support growing demand.

enriched_points.point_title: California as a leader in hydrogen infrastructure
enriched_points.expanded_content: California leads in hydrogen infrastructure development, hosting 54 retail stations, facilitating the adoption of fuel cell electric vehicles and supporting a growing market.
Safety and efficiency are critical challenges for hydrogen stations, requiring adherence to stringent safety codes and standards to ensure safe fueling practices for consumers.
Hydrogen fueling stations consist of storage tanks, dispensers, and infrastructure for hydrogen production, with ongoing innovations including mobile fueling solutions for wider access.
Other states are poised for growth in hydrogen infrastructure, supported by federal initiatives and increasing demand for hydrogen-powered vehicles beyond California.
